expanded_description: A clear introduction to transpiration for secondary school biology. This covers how water moves from roots to leaves and evaporates into the air, the physical forces that pull water up tall trees, how plants control water loss, and why transpiration is both essential and costly for plants.

core_concept.principle: Transpiration is the loss of water vapor from plant leaves. It creates a suction force that pulls water up from the roots through the xylem. This process is driven by the sun, not by the plant's energy.
core_concept.explanation:
Plants need water for photosynthesis, to keep their cells firm (turgid), and to transport minerals from the soil. But they also lose water constantly through their leaves. This water loss is called transpiration.

When water evaporates from the inside of a leaf, it creates a suction. This suction pulls more water out of the xylem (the plant's water pipes). The suction travels all the way down to the roots, pulling water up from the soil. This is how water gets to the top of a 100-meter tree—the leaves are pulling it up, not the roots pushing it.

Transpiration happens mostly through stomata—the tiny pores on the underside of leaves. When stomata are open, carbon dioxide enters for photosynthesis, but water vapor also escapes. When they are closed, water loss stops, but so does photosynthesis. This is the plant's biggest trade-off: it must lose water to get carbon dioxide.

The physical forces that make this work are called cohesion and adhesion. Cohesion means water molecules stick to each other (like magnets). Adhesion means water molecules stick to the walls of the xylem. Together, these forces keep the water column from breaking as it is pulled upward.

Transpiration has costs and benefits. The cost is water loss—a large tree can lose hundreds of liters of water per day. The benefits are that it moves water and minerals to the leaves, it cools the leaves (like sweating), and it keeps the plant's cells firm.

Plants control transpiration by opening and closing their stomata. They open when there is plenty of water, light for photosynthesis, and not too much heat. They close when water is scarce, the air is dry, or it is too hot. This balance is essential for survival.

Understanding transpiration helps us know when to water crops, how trees survive drought, and how climate affects plant growth.

image.alt: A diagram showing water evaporating from leaves, creating suction that pulls water up through the xylem from roots to leaves
image.caption: Transpiration: Water evaporates from leaves → creates suction → pulls water up through xylem → water moves from roots to leaves. Driven by the sun, not by the plant.

sections.introduction:
Imagine you are a giant redwood tree, 100 meters tall. Your leaves are high in the sky, catching sunlight. Your roots are deep in the soil, soaking up water. How does water get from your roots to your leaves? There is no heart at the bottom to pump it up. There is no pump anywhere. Yet water flows upward—hundreds of liters a day.

The answer is transpiration. Water evaporates from your leaves, and that evaporation pulls water up from the roots. It is like sucking on a straw. Your leaves are doing the sucking. The sun provides the energy.

Transpiration is the loss of water vapor from plant leaves. It sounds like a waste—and it is, in a way. A large oak tree can lose 40,000 liters of water on a hot summer day. That water is gone, evaporated into the air. But this "waste" is also the engine that drives water movement through the plant.

Without transpiration, water would not reach the leaves. Minerals from the soil would not be delivered. The plant would wilt and die. Transpiration is essential, even though it costs water.

Transpiration also cools the plant. When water evaporates, it takes heat with it. This is why plants don't overheat in the sun. A leaf can be 5-10°C cooler than the air around it because of transpiration.

Plants have to balance the benefits of transpiration against the cost of water loss. They control this balance with stomata—tiny pores that can open and close. When water is plentiful, stomata open, letting carbon dioxide in for photosynthesis and water vapor out. When water is scarce, stomata close, saving water but stopping photosynthesis.

Understanding transpiration helps us understand how plants work, how to water crops efficiently, and how forests affect the climate. It also helps us appreciate the clever design of leaves—built to capture light and exchange gases, but also to control water loss.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing a tree losing water vapor, a corn plant losing water, and a global map of transpiration
introduction_image.caption: Transpiration scale: A single corn plant loses 200 liters in a season; a large oak loses 40,000 liters on a hot day; all plants together move 70,000 cubic kilometers of water per year—10% of the water in the atmosphere.

key_concept.definition: Transpiration is the evaporation of water from plant leaves. It creates a suction that pulls water up through the xylem from the roots. The water column is held together by cohesion (water sticks to water) and adhesion (water sticks to xylem walls).
key_concept.context:
Most transpiration happens through stomata (90-95%). A small amount (5-10%) happens through the cuticle (waxy coating). The rate of transpiration depends on environmental factors (light, temperature, humidity, wind) and plant factors (stomatal opening, leaf area, root depth).

Water moves from soil to roots to stem to leaves to air. This is called the transpiration stream. It is driven by water potential—water moves from high water potential (wet soil) to low water potential (dry air). The gradient from soil to air is huge, which is why water flows so fast.

Cohesion-tension theory explains how water can be pulled up against gravity. Water molecules are cohesive (they stick to each other by hydrogen bonds). When one water molecule is pulled up, it pulls the one below it. This creates a continuous column from roots to leaves. Adhesion (water sticking to xylem walls) helps keep the column from slipping.

Transpiration is passive—it uses no energy from the plant. The energy comes from the sun, which heats the leaves and causes evaporation. This is why plants need sunlight not just for photosynthesis, but also for water transport.

image.alt: A diagram showing the transpiration stream: water moves from soil into roots, up through xylem in the stem, into leaves, and out through stomata
image.caption: Transpiration stream: Soil water → root hairs → root xylem → stem xylem → leaf veins → mesophyll cells → stomata → atmosphere. Cohesion and adhesion keep the water column intact.

sections.key_concept: Transpiration is driven by the sun and explained by the cohesion-tension theory. Water evaporates from leaves, creating tension that pulls water up through xylem. Cohesion and adhesion keep the water column from breaking.

classifications.value: Understanding cohesion-tension explains how water can move to the top of a 100-meter tree without a pump.
characteristics: Cohesion: Water molecules stick to each other. Hydrogen bonds between water molecules create a strong cohesive force (up to 30 MPa).
Adhesion: Water molecules stick to the walls of xylem vessels. The walls are hydrophilic (water-loving), so water spreads and clings.
Tension: Evaporation from leaves creates negative pressure (tension) in the xylem. This tension can be -1 to -3 MPa (megapascals) in well-watered plants, and even lower in drought.
Continuous column: The water column from roots to leaves is unbroken. If it breaks (cavitation), water flow stops until the column is repaired.
Passive process: The energy comes from the sun (evaporation), not from the plant's metabolism.
classifications.behavior:
Imagine a rope made of water. The rope is attached to the roots at one end and to the leaves at the other. When water evaporates from the leaves, it pulls on the rope. The rope is strong because water molecules are cohesive. It does not slip because water is adhesive to the xylem walls. The rope is pulled upward, and the entire column moves.

This is the cohesion-tension theory. It explains how water can rise 100 meters or more. The tension in the xylem can be measured with a pressure bomb. A leaf is cut and placed in a chamber. Pressure is applied until water appears at the cut end. The pressure needed equals the tension in the xylem. Typical values: -0.5 MPa in the morning, -2.0 MPa at midday, -0.5 MPa at night.

The cohesion-tension theory also explains why trees shrink during the day. When tension is high, the xylem vessels contract slightly. A tree trunk can shrink by 1-2 millimeters during the day and expand again at night. This is measured with dendrometers.

If tension gets too high (during drought), the water column can break. An air bubble forms (cavitation) and the vessel is blocked (embolism). This is like an air bubble in a straw—you can't suck water past it. Plants can sometimes repair embolized vessels at night when tension is low, or they grow new xylem.

examples.process: The Pressure Bomb: Measuring Xylem Tension
examples.explanation:
How do we know that xylem water is under tension? The pressure bomb experiment proves it. Take a leaf or small branch. Cut it and place the cut end through a rubber seal into a pressure chamber. The leaf is outside the chamber; the cut end is inside. Then, increase the pressure in the chamber. When the pressure equals the tension in the xylem, water will appear at the cut end. The pressure needed to make water appear is equal to the tension.

In a well-watered plant, the tension might be -0.5 MPa at dawn and -2.0 MPa at midday. In a drought-stressed plant, it might be -3.0 MPa or lower. If the tension gets too high (more negative than about -4 MPa), the water column can break (cavitation).

This experiment confirms that xylem water is under tension, not pressure. If it were under pressure, water would squirt out when the stem is cut. Instead, it sucks in. This is one of the key pieces of evidence for the cohesion-tension theory.

image.alt: A diagram of a pressure bomb apparatus with a leaf inside, and pressure gauge showing tension values
image.caption: Pressure bomb: Leaf placed in chamber → pressure increased until water appears at cut end → pressure equals xylem tension. Values: -0.5 MPa (dawn) to -2.0 MPa (midday) to -3.0+ MPa (drought).

examples.process: Tree Trunk Shrinks During the Day
examples.explanation:
Have you ever noticed that trees seem to shrink on hot days? They do. The trunk diameter changes throughout the day because of xylem tension. At night, when transpiration is low, tension is low, and the trunk expands. During the day, when transpiration is high, tension is high, and the trunk contracts.

This can be measured with a dendrometer—a device that wraps around the trunk and measures changes in circumference. The daily change can be 1-2 millimeters for a large tree. That may not sound like much, but it is a physical proof that the xylem is under tension.

During drought, the daily shrinkage is larger because tension is higher. After rain, the trunk expands again as tension drops. This is why trees can "pump" water—the tension pulls water up, and the trunk contracts slightly.

image.alt: A graph showing trunk diameter decreasing during the day and increasing at night, with an inset of a dendrometer on a tree
image.caption: Stem diameter: Night (low tension, expanded) → Day (high tension, contracted). Daily change 1-2 mm. Physical proof that xylem is under tension during transpiration.

classifications.value: Understanding water potential explains why water moves from soil to leaves to air.
characteristics: Water potential (Ψ) measures the free energy of water. Water moves from high Ψ to low Ψ.
Typical values: Soil: -0.1 to -1.5 MPa; Root: -0.2 to -2.0 MPa; Stem: -0.5 to -2.5 MPa; Leaf: -1.0 to -3.0 MPa; Air: -100 MPa (at 50% humidity).
The gradient from soil to air is huge—about 100 MPa. This drives water flow.
The gradient can be reduced by drought (soil Ψ becomes more negative) or by stomatal closure (leaf Ψ becomes less negative).
classifications.behavior:
Water always moves from high water potential to low water potential. It is like water flowing downhill. The "hill" is the water potential gradient.

At the top of the hill is the soil. Wet soil has high water potential (close to 0 MPa). Dry soil has low water potential (very negative). At the bottom of the hill is the atmosphere. Dry air has very low water potential (-100 MPa or lower).

Water moves down this hill: from soil to roots to stem to leaves to air. The steepest part of the hill is between the leaf and the air. That is where the driving force is strongest.

Plants can change the slope of the hill. When they open their stomata, they lower leaf water potential (more negative), steepening the slope and increasing flow. When they close their stomata, they raise leaf water potential (less negative), flattening the slope and reducing flow.

When soil dries, the top of the hill (soil) gets lower. The slope flattens, and flow slows. The plant may close its stomata to conserve water, but this also flattens the slope. The plant must balance the need for water (open stomata) against the risk of running out (closed stomata).

examples.process: How Drought Affects the Water Potential Gradient
examples.explanation:
Imagine a well-watered plant. Soil Ψ = -0.1 MPa. Leaf Ψ = -1.5 MPa. Gradient = 1.4 MPa. Water flows fast. The plant can photosynthesize at high rates.

Now imagine a drought. Soil Ψ drops to -1.0 MPa. If the leaf Ψ stayed at -1.5 MPa, the gradient would be only 0.5 MPa. Flow would be much slower. The plant would struggle to get enough water.

But the plant can respond. It can close its stomata, which reduces transpiration and allows leaf Ψ to rise. If leaf Ψ rises to -1.2 MPa, the gradient is 0.2 MPa—even smaller. Flow is slower, but water loss is reduced. The plant is conserving water.

If the drought continues, the plant may let leaf Ψ drop to -2.0 MPa or lower, steepening the gradient but risking cavitation. This is a dangerous game. If the soil gets too dry, the gradient may become negative (soil Ψ lower than leaf Ψ), and water will flow out of the plant. The plant will wilt.

image.alt: Diagrams showing water potential from soil to leaf under well-watered and drought conditions
image.caption: Water potential gradient: Well-watered (soil -0.1, leaf -1.5, gradient 1.4) → fast flow. Drought (soil -1.0, leaf -1.2, gradient 0.2) → slow flow. Plant closes stomata to conserve water.

classifications.value: Understanding environmental factors explains why plants lose more water on hot, dry, windy days.
characteristics: Light: Stomata open in light, close in dark. Light also heats leaves, increasing evaporation.
Temperature: Higher temperature increases evaporation rate and increases the air's capacity to hold water (vapor pressure).
Humidity: Dry air (low humidity) increases the gradient for water vapor to leave the leaf. Humid air decreases it.
Wind: Wind removes the boundary layer (still air around the leaf), increasing transpiration. Very strong wind may cause stomata to close.
Soil water: When soil is dry, plants close stomata to conserve water, reducing transpiration.
classifications.behavior:
Transpiration is like water evaporating from a wet sponge. On a hot, dry, windy day, the sponge dries quickly. On a cool, humid, calm day, it dries slowly. Leaves are the same.

Light has a big effect. Stomata open in response to blue light (a signal that the sun is up). They also open in response to low CO₂ inside the leaf (which happens when photosynthesis uses up CO₂). So during the day, stomata are open and transpiration is high. At night, they close and transpiration is low.

Temperature affects transpiration in two ways. First, warm air can hold more water vapor than cool air. So when air warms, the vapor pressure deficit (the difference between the saturated vapor pressure inside the leaf and the actual vapor pressure outside) increases. This drives more transpiration. Second, temperature affects the rate of evaporation directly—hotter water evaporates faster.

Humidity is the most direct factor. When the air is dry, water vapor leaves the leaf quickly. When the air is humid, water vapor leaves slowly. This is why plants wilt on dry, windy days but not on humid days.

Wind removes the boundary layer—the thin layer of still air that clings to the leaf. In still air, water vapor builds up in this layer, reducing the gradient. Wind blows this layer away, increasing transpiration. This is why plants near a fan dry out faster.

Soil water is the ultimate control. No matter how favorable the conditions, if the soil is dry, plants will close their stomata to save water. Transpiration will be low.

examples.process: Vapor Pressure Deficit (VPD): The Driver of Transpiration
examples.explanation:
Vapor pressure deficit (VPD) is the difference between how much water vapor the air can hold when saturated (at leaf temperature) and how much it actually holds. It is a measure of how "thirsty" the air is.

At 25°C, saturated air contains 3.17 kPa of water vapor. If the relative humidity is 50%, the actual vapor pressure is 1.58 kPa. VPD = 3.17 - 1.58 = 1.59 kPa. This VPD drives transpiration.

If the temperature rises to 35°C, saturated vapor pressure is 5.63 kPa. If humidity stays at 50%, actual vapor pressure is 2.81 kPa. VPD = 5.63 - 2.81 = 2.82 kPa. Transpiration nearly doubles.

If the temperature is 25°C but humidity drops to 20%, actual vapor pressure is 0.63 kPa. VPD = 3.17 - 0.63 = 2.54 kPa. Transpiration is high even though temperature is moderate.

Plants close stomata when VPD gets too high (usually >2.0-2.5 kPa) to prevent excessive water loss. This is why leaves often wilt on hot, dry afternoons.

image.alt: A graph showing VPD and transpiration rate, with examples for different temperature and humidity combinations
image.caption: VPD effect: Low VPD (cool, humid) → low transpiration. High VPD (hot, dry) → high transpiration. VPD >2.0 kPa triggers stomatal closure to prevent water loss.

principles.principle: Plants Face a Trade-off Between Carbon Gain and Water Loss
principles.statement: Stomata must open to let CO₂ in for photosynthesis, but open stomata also let water vapor out. This is the fundamental trade-off of plant life.
principles.application:
When stomata are open, the plant gains carbon (good) but loses water (bad). When stomata are closed, it saves water (good) but cannot photosynthesize (bad). The plant must decide when to open and when to close.

Water-use efficiency (WUE) measures how much carbon a plant gains per water lost. High WUE means the plant gets more carbon for each unit of water. C₄ plants (corn, sugarcane) have higher WUE than C₃ plants (wheat, rice). CAM plants (cacti, succulents) have the highest WUE.

Plants optimize WUE by opening stomata when conditions favor photosynthesis (high light, moderate temperature) and closing them when conditions favor water loss (drought, high VPD). They also adjust stomatal density over the long term—fewer stomata in dry environments, more in wet environments.

Breeders are trying to improve WUE in crops. By selecting plants that get more carbon per water lost, they can grow more food with less water. This is especially important in regions with drought.
principles.example:
Compare a corn plant (C₄) and a wheat plant (C₃) on a hot, dry day. The corn plant can keep its stomata partly closed and still photosynthesize because it concentrates CO₂ in its bundle sheath cells. The wheat plant must keep its stomata wide open to get enough CO₂. On a hot day, the wheat plant loses much more water per carbon gained.

This is why corn is more drought-tolerant than wheat. Corn's WUE is about twice that of wheat. In regions with limited water, farmers grow corn rather than wheat because it produces more food per drop of water.
principles.analogy: Stomata are like a business. The business needs to bring in raw materials (CO₂) to make products (sugar). But bringing in raw materials costs money (water). The business must decide how much to spend based on how much money it has (soil water) and how much demand there is (light). A C₄ plant is like a business with better technology—it can make the same products with less spending.

image.alt: A graph showing stomatal conductance vs. photosynthesis and transpiration, with arrows showing the trade-off
image.caption: Trade-off: Open stomata → high CO₂ (good) + high water loss (bad). Closed stomata → low water loss (good) + low CO₂ (bad). WUE = photosynthesis / transpiration. C₄ plants have higher WUE.

principles.principle: Xylem Structure Affects Transport Efficiency and Safety
principles.statement: Wide xylem vessels move water fast but are vulnerable to air bubbles (cavitation). Narrow vessels are safer but slower. Plants balance these trade-offs based on their environment.
principles.application:
The rate of water flow through a xylem vessel depends on its radius to the fourth power (r⁴). A vessel twice as wide can carry 16 times as much water. Wide vessels are efficient, but they are also vulnerable to cavitation. When water is pulled too hard, an air bubble can form, blocking the vessel.

Plants in wet environments have wide vessels to move water quickly. They can afford to be vulnerable because water is always available. Plants in dry environments have narrow vessels that resist cavitation. They are slower, but they can survive drought.

Ring-porous trees (like oak) have wide vessels in the spring (for fast growth) and narrow vessels in the summer (for safety). Diffuse-porous trees (like maple) have uniform vessels all year—moderate efficiency and safety.

This trade-off explains why some trees are more drought-tolerant than others. It also explains why trees die in droughts—their xylem cavitates, and they cannot move water to their leaves.
principles.example: Oak trees have wide vessels in their earlywood. In a wet spring, they can grow quickly, moving lots of water to new leaves. But if a drought hits in summer, those wide vessels cavitate easily. The oak may lose many of its leaves to save water. Maple trees have narrower vessels. They grow more slowly, but they can survive drought better. This is why maples are often found in drier sites than oaks.
principles.analogy: Xylem vessels are like highways. Wide highways (wide vessels) let traffic flow fast, but one accident (cavitation) can cause a huge jam. Narrow highways (narrow vessels) are slower, but accidents have less impact. In a city with lots of traffic (wet environment), you build wide highways. In a rural area (dry environment), narrow roads are fine.

image.alt: Diagrams of wide vessels (high conductivity, high cavitation risk) and narrow vessels (low conductivity, low risk)
image.caption: Efficiency vs. safety: Wide vessels (fast flow, high cavitation risk) → wet environments. Narrow vessels (slow flow, low risk) → dry environments. Ring-porous trees (oak) have both.

principles.principle: Stomata Integrate Multiple Signals to Balance Water Loss and Carbon Gain
principles.statement: Stomata do not just open and close randomly. They respond to light, CO₂, humidity, temperature, and water stress, integrating all these signals to decide how open to be.
principles.application:
Guard cells are like tiny computers. They have sensors for light (blue light receptors), CO₂ (unknown sensor), water stress (ABA hormone), and temperature. They also have a circadian clock that anticipates day and night.

When light is strong, the guard cells get a signal to open. But if CO₂ is high, they get a signal to close (the plant already has enough CO₂). If the air is dry, they get a signal to close (to save water). If the roots sense dry soil, they send ABA, which tells the guard cells to close. The guard cells weigh all these signals and decide how open to be.

This integration allows the plant to optimize its stomatal aperture for current conditions. On a sunny, humid morning, stomata open wide. On a hot, dry afternoon, they close. This is why plants don't just open at dawn and close at dusk—they respond to the environment.
principles.example: During a drought, roots produce ABA. This hormone travels up the xylem to the leaves. It reaches the guard cells and binds to receptors. This triggers a cascade that causes the guard cells to release potassium, lose water, and close. This happens even if the leaves are still well-watered. The plant is responding to the soil, not to its own leaves. This is an early warning system—the plant closes stomata before it runs out of water.
principles.analogy: Guard cells are like a thermostat. A thermostat has sensors for temperature, humidity, and time of day. It integrates these signals to decide when to turn the heat on or off. Guard cells do the same thing for stomata. They sense light, CO₂, humidity, and water stress, and they decide when to open or close.

image.alt: A diagram showing guard cells with inputs from light, CO₂, humidity, and ABA, and output of open or closed
image.caption: Stomatal integration: Guard cells sense light (open), CO₂ (close if high), humidity (close if dry), ABA (close under drought). They weigh all signals to set aperture.

sections.section_title: Key Components of the Transpiration Stream

components.definition: The transpiration stream has several components: soil water uptake, root transport, xylem ascent, leaf distribution, and atmospheric evaporation. Each component has resistance that affects the overall flow rate.
components.interpretation: Water flow through the plant is like electrical current. The water potential gradient is like voltage—it drives flow. The resistances (soil, root, xylem, leaf, stomata) are like resistors. The plant can control stomatal resistance, but not soil or xylem resistance. This is why stomata are the main control point for transpiration.

image.alt: A diagram of the transpiration stream showing resistances at soil, root, xylem, leaf, and stomata
image.caption: Transpiration stream: Soil (Rₛ) → Root (Rᵣ) → Xylem (Rₓ) → Leaf (Rₗ) → Stomata (Rₛₜ) → Air. Flow = ΔΨ / ΣR. Stomata control the main resistance.

categories.description: Water enters roots through root hairs, driven by water potential gradient and facilitated by aquaporins (water channels).
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Roots absorb water through root hairs—tiny extensions of epidermal cells that greatly increase surface area. Water enters the root by osmosis, moving from soil (higher water potential) into the root (lower water potential).

Water can take two paths through the root. The apoplastic path goes through cell walls (fast but non-selective). The symplastic path goes through cells (slower but selective). At the endodermis, the Casparian strip blocks the apoplastic path, forcing water to go through the symplastic path. This allows the plant to select which minerals enter the xylem.

Aquaporins are water channel proteins in cell membranes. They increase the permeability of membranes to water by 10-100 times. Plants can regulate aquaporins. Under drought, they close aquaporins to reduce water loss from roots to dry soil. Under high transpiration, they open aquaporins to maximize uptake.

Roots can also grow deeper to reach water. When the topsoil dries, plants send roots deeper. This is a long-term adaptation to drought.

examples.explanation:
Imagine a root cell membrane. Without aquaporins, water crosses slowly by diffusion. With aquaporins, water rushes through like water through a pipe. A membrane with aquaporins can be 100 times more permeable to water.

Plants can open and close aquaporins. When the plant needs lots of water (sunny day), it opens them. When water is scarce (drought), it closes them. This is done by phosphorylation (adding a phosphate group) which opens the channel, or dephosphorylation which closes it.

ABA also closes aquaporins. When the roots sense dry soil, they produce ABA. This hormone signals the aquaporins to close, reducing water uptake. This prevents water from flowing out of the roots into dry soil (reverse osmosis).

Plants without functional aquaporins grow poorly under high transpiration but normally under low transpiration. This shows that aquaporins are essential for meeting high water demand.

image.alt: A diagram of aquaporin water channels in a root cell membrane, opening and closing
image.caption: Aquaporins: Water channels increase membrane permeability 10-100×. Open under high transpiration (maximize uptake), closed under drought (prevent water loss). Regulated by ABA.

categories.description: Xylem vessels conduct water under tension. Cavitation (air bubbles) can block flow and reduce transport capacity.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Xylem vessels are hollow tubes made of dead cells. They are perfect for conducting water because there is nothing inside to block flow. But they are vulnerable to cavitation—the formation of air bubbles under high tension.

Cavitation happens when the tension becomes so high that the water column breaks. An air bubble forms and expands, filling the vessel. This vessel is now embolized—it cannot conduct water. The plant can sometimes repair embolized vessels at night when tension is low, or it can grow new vessels.

The vulnerability to cavitation depends on the structure of the pits (openings in the vessel walls). Plants with small pit pores are more resistant to cavitation because air bubbles cannot pass through. Conifers have torus-margo pits that seal when pressure changes, preventing the spread of air bubbles.

Drought kills trees when cavitation becomes widespread. If too many xylem vessels are embolized, the plant cannot get enough water to its leaves. The leaves die, and the plant may not recover.

examples.explanation:
Imagine a straw with water in it. If you suck too hard, an air bubble forms. You can't suck past the bubble. The straw is blocked. The same thing happens in xylem vessels.

Cavitation happens when the tension exceeds the water's ability to stay liquid. This is more likely when water is under tension for a long time (drought) or when water freezes and thaws (winter). Air bubbles can also enter through pit membranes (air-seeding).

Once a vessel is embolized, it is blocked. The plant must rely on other vessels. If many vessels become blocked, the plant's ability to transport water decreases. This is called hydraulic failure. It is one of the main ways drought kills trees.

Some plants are more resistant to cavitation than others. Drought-tolerant species have narrow vessels with small pit pores. They can survive tensions of -4 MPa or more. Drought-sensitive species have wide vessels and may cavitate at -2 MPa.

image.alt: A diagram showing an air bubble forming in a xylem vessel (cavitation) and blocking flow
image.caption: Cavitation: High tension → air bubble forms → vessel embolized → water flow blocked. Plants with narrow vessels resist cavitation better. Hydraulic failure kills trees in drought.

worked_examples.title: Understanding Transpiration and Water-Use Efficiency
worked_examples.problem: A plant has a soil water potential of -0.2 MPa and a leaf water potential of -1.2 MPa. The total resistance to water flow is 10 MPa·s·mmol⁻¹. The photosynthetic rate is 10 µmol CO₂/m²/s, and the stomatal conductance is 0.15 mol H₂O/m²/s. (a) Calculate the transpiration rate (E = ΔΨ / R). (b) Calculate the intrinsic water-use efficiency (WUEᵢ = A / gₛ). (c) If drought lowers soil Ψ to -0.8 MPa and the plant closes its stomata (gₛ = 0.05, A = 4), what is the new E and WUEᵢ? (d) What does this tell us about the plant's response to drought?

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate transpiration rate under well-watered conditions.
step_by_step.calculation: ΔΨ = Ψₛₒᵢₗ - Ψₗₑₐf = -0.2 - (-1.2) = 1.0 MPa. E = ΔΨ / R = 1.0 / 10 = 0.1 mmol H₂O/m²/s.
WUEᵢ = A / gₛ = 10 / 0.15 = 66.7 µmol CO₂/mol H₂O.
New ΔΨ = -0.8 - (-1.2) = 0.4 MPa. E = 0.4 / 10 = 0.04 mmol H₂O/m²/s. New WUEᵢ = 4 / 0.05 = 80 µmol CO₂/mol H₂O.

Transpiration dropped from 0.1 to 0.04 (60% reduction). Photosynthesis dropped from 10 to 4 (60% reduction). WUEᵢ increased from 66.7 to 80 (20% increase). The plant saved as much water as it lost carbon, and actually became more water-efficient. This is why plants close stomata in drought—the small loss in photosynthesis is worth the large saving in water.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) E = 0.1 mmol/m²/s. (b) WUEᵢ = 66.7 µmol/mol. (c) Drought: E = 0.04, WUEᵢ = 80. (d) The plant saves water by closing stomata; photosynthesis decreases but water-use efficiency increases. This trade-off allows the plant to survive drought.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Transpiration rate depends on the water potential gradient and resistance. Water-use efficiency (WUE) measures carbon gained per water lost. In drought, plants close stomata, reducing both transpiration and photosynthesis, but WUE often increases.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: The Journey of a Water Molecule
sections.introduction: A water molecule travels from the soil, through the plant, and into the air. Understanding this journey reveals how transpiration works.

process_flow.description: Water moves from soil into root hairs by osmosis, driven by the water potential gradient.
process_flow.mechanism: Soil water (Ψ ≈ -0.1 MPa) enters root hairs. Aquaporins in root cell membranes increase water flow. Water moves through the cortex via apoplastic (cell walls) or symplastic (through cells) pathways. The Casparian strip in the endodermis blocks the apoplastic path, forcing water into the symplastic path where selective uptake occurs.
process_flow.result: Water enters xylem in the root.

image.alt: Water moving from soil into root hairs, through cortex, and into xylem
image.caption: Step 1: Water enters root hairs (osmosis) → moves through cortex → passes endodermis (Casparian strip) → enters xylem. Aquaporins regulate flow.

process_flow.description: Water moves upward through xylem vessels, pulled by tension from the leaves.
process_flow.mechanism: Xylem water (Ψ ≈ -0.5 to -2.5 MPa) is under tension. Cohesion (water sticks to water) keeps the column intact; adhesion (water sticks to xylem walls) prevents slippage. Water moves through pits between vessels. Flow rate depends on vessel diameter (wider = faster).

image.alt: Water moving up xylem vessels, with cohesion and adhesion forces shown
image.caption: Step 2: Water pulled up xylem by tension. Cohesion (water sticks to water) + Adhesion (water sticks to walls) keep column intact. Flow rate depends on vessel diameter.

process_flow.description: Water moves from xylem to mesophyll cells and evaporates into air spaces, then exits through stomata.
process_flow.mechanism: Water leaves xylem veins → enters mesophyll cells → evaporates into intercellular air spaces → diffuses through stomata → into atmosphere (Ψ ≈ -100 MPa). Evaporation creates tension, pulling more water from xylem. Stomata regulate the rate: open (high flow), closed (low flow).
process_flow.result: Water lost to atmosphere; tension maintained.

image.alt: Water evaporating from mesophyll cells and exiting through stomata
image.caption: Step 3: Water evaporates from mesophyll → air spaces → stomata → atmosphere. Evaporation creates tension that pulls water from xylem. Stomata regulate rate.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Plant Adaptations to Manage Transpiration
sections.definition: Plants have evolved many ways to reduce water loss and survive drought. These include structural adaptations (thick cuticles, sunken stomata, leaf rolling) and physiological adaptations (stomatal control, hydraulic adjustment).

process_types.energy_profile: Drought adaptations reduce photosynthesis but increase survival. Plants trade growth for survival.
process_types.direction: Well-watered: high transpiration, fast growth → Drought: low transpiration, survival mode
process_types.function: Minimize water loss to survive dry periods
goals: Roll leaves to trap moist air
Grow deeper roots to access water
Store water in stems or leaves (succulence)
process_types.energy_source: Reduced photosynthesis during drought; stored carbohydrates sustain metabolism.

primary_example.explanation:
When a grass plant gets dry, its leaves roll up. This is caused by bulliform cells—large, thin-walled cells on the upper surface of the leaf. When the plant is well-watered, these cells are full and the leaf is flat. When the plant is dry, the bulliform cells lose water and shrink. This causes the leaf to curl inward.

Why does the leaf roll? When the leaf is rolled, the stomata (which are on the lower surface) are inside the tube. The air inside the tube becomes humid from transpiration, so the gradient for water loss is smaller. The rolled leaf also has less surface area exposed to the sun and wind.

Leaf rolling is reversible. When it rains, the bulliform cells fill with water again, and the leaf unrolls. This allows the plant to photosynthesize fully when water is available and to conserve water when it is not.

image.alt: A grass leaf flat (well-watered) and rolled (dry), with an inset showing bulliform cells
image.caption: Leaf rolling: Bulliform cells lose water → shrink → leaf curls. Stomata inside tube, reducing water loss. Reversible when water returns.

image.alt: Multiple drought adaptations: thick cuticle, sunken stomata, leaf rolling, succulent leaves
image.caption: Drought adaptations: Thick cuticle (reduces water loss), Sunken stomata (trap moist air), Leaf rolling (reduces exposure), Succulence (stores water).

process_types.energy_profile: ABA signaling triggers stomatal closure under drought. This saves water but reduces photosynthesis.
process_types.direction: Well-watered (stomata open) → ABA signal → Stomata close → Water saved
process_types.function: Prevent excessive water loss when soil is dry
goals: Transport ABA to leaves via xylem
Guard cells close stomata in response to ABA
process_types.energy_source: ABA synthesis requires energy; stomatal closure reduces energy capture (photosynthesis).

primary_example.explanation:
When soil dries, plant roots produce a hormone called abscisic acid (ABA). ABA travels up the xylem to the leaves. There, it binds to receptors in guard cells. This triggers a cascade of events that causes the guard cells to release potassium, lose water, and close.

ABA is an early warning system. It closes stomata before the leaves themselves get dry. This prevents water loss before it becomes dangerous. The plant sacrifices photosynthesis to save water.

When it rains, ABA production stops. Existing ABA is broken down. The guard cells take up potassium again, and the stomata reopen. The plant resumes photosynthesis.

This system allows plants to respond quickly to drought. Within hours of soil drying, stomata close. Within hours of rain, they reopen. This is how plants balance water conservation with carbon gain.

image.alt: ABA produced in roots, transported to leaves, binding to guard cell receptors and closing stomata
image.caption: ABA: Roots sense dry soil → produce ABA → ABA travels to leaves → guard cells close stomata. Early warning system saves water before leaves dry out.

image.alt: Guard cells open (well-watered) and closed (ABA present)
image.caption: ABA effect: ABA binds guard cell receptors → K⁺ exits → water exits → guard cells relax → stomata close. Conserves water under drought.

sections.relationship: Drought adaptations and ABA signaling work together. ABA triggers short-term responses (stomatal closure). Structural adaptations (thick cuticle, leaf rolling) provide long-term protection. Both help plants survive dry periods.

sections.purpose: These are the essential vocabulary terms for understanding transpiration. Knowing these terms will help you describe how water moves through plants and how plants control water loss.

activation.definition: The terms below cover the most important concepts in transpiration. Each term is defined simply, with context to help you remember its significance.
activation.effect: Mastering these terms will allow you to discuss plant water relations, drought responses, and the cohesion-tension theory with confidence.

image.alt: A labeled diagram of a plant showing transpiration stream, stomata, xylem, and water potential gradient
image.caption: Key transpiration terms: Knowing these concepts helps you understand how water moves through plants and how they control water loss.

key_properties.explanation: The loss of water vapor from plant leaves, primarily through stomata. Creates tension that pulls water up from roots.

The explanation for how water moves up xylem. Transpiration creates tension that pulls the water column upward. Cohesion (water sticks to water) and adhesion (water sticks to xylem walls) keep the column intact.
Water molecules stick to each other by hydrogen bonds. This creates a continuous water column from roots to leaves.
Water molecules stick to the walls of xylem vessels. This prevents the water column from slipping and helps maintain tension.
A measure of the free energy of water. Water moves from high water potential (less negative) to low water potential (more negative). Soil Ψ > root Ψ > stem Ψ > leaf Ψ > air Ψ drives transpiration.
Negative pressure in the xylem caused by transpiration. Ranges from -0.5 MPa (well-watered) to -3.0 MPa or lower (drought).
Pores in the leaf epidermis that allow gas exchange. Each stoma is surrounded by two guard cells that open and close the pore.
Specialized cells that surround each stoma. Take up K⁺ to open (water enters, cells swell) and release K⁺ to close (water exits, cells relax).
The difference between saturated vapor pressure (at leaf temperature) and actual vapor pressure (in the air). A measure of how "thirsty" the air is. Higher VPD = higher transpiration.
The formation of an air bubble in xylem under high tension. Blocks water flow (embolism). Can be caused by drought or freezing.
An air-filled xylem vessel that no longer conducts water. Result of cavitation.
A plant hormone that causes stomatal closure under drought. Produced in roots when soil is dry, transported to leaves.
The amount of carbon gained per amount of water lost. Higher WUE = more efficient water use. C₄ plants have higher WUE than C₃ plants.
The thin layer of still air that clings to the leaf surface. Water vapor builds up here, reducing transpiration. Wind removes the boundary layer, increasing transpiration.
Positive pressure generated in roots by active ion pumping into xylem. Can push water upward, especially at night. Causes guttation (water droplets on leaf edges).
Water droplets that form on leaf edges in the morning. Caused by root pressure pushing water out of hydathodes (special pores). Occurs when transpiration is low (humid nights).
Water channel protein in cell membranes. Increases membrane permeability to water by 10-100×. Can be opened or closed in response to signals.
A waterproof band in the endodermal cell walls. Blocks the apoplastic pathway, forcing water to go through the symplastic pathway. Allows selective uptake of minerals.
A drought response where leaves curl inward. Reduces exposed surface area and traps moist air, reducing transpiration. Caused by bulliform cells losing turgor.
Large, thin-walled cells on the upper surface of grass leaves. Lose water under drought, causing leaf to curl. Regain water when rain returns, leaf unrolls.

summary:
Transpiration is the evaporation of water from plant leaves. It creates tension that pulls water up through the xylem from the roots. This process is explained by the cohesion-tension theory. Water molecules are cohesive (they stick to each other) and adhesive (they stick to xylem walls), forming a continuous column from roots to leaves. When water evaporates from leaves, it pulls on this column, and the tension travels all the way down to the roots.

Transpiration is driven by the sun, not by the plant's energy. The water potential gradient from soil to air is huge—about 100 MPa. This gradient drives water flow. The rate of transpiration depends on environmental factors: light (stomata open), temperature (higher = more evaporation), humidity (lower = more evaporation), and wind (removes boundary layer).

Stomata are the main control points. Guard cells open and close the stomata in response to light, CO₂, humidity, and the hormone ABA (which signals drought). When stomata are open, CO₂ enters for photosynthesis, but water vapor escapes. This is the fundamental trade-off of plant life: carbon gain vs. water loss.

Plants have many adaptations to manage transpiration. In drought, they close stomata, roll leaves, and grow deeper roots. C₄ plants (corn) have higher water-use efficiency than C₃ plants (wheat). CAM plants (cacti) open stomata at night to save water.

Understanding transpiration helps us know when to water crops, how trees survive drought, and how climate affects plant growth. It also reveals the elegant physics behind water movement in plants—a 100-meter column of water, pulled by the sun, held together by the invisible force of hydrogen bonds.
